figures.caption: Figure 1: Density-stratified coordinate transformation for the two cooled Zhang-Duan-Choudhari calibration flat plates (Zhang et al. 2018). (a) Wall-normal profiles of 𝜌+ and 𝜇+ that form the ordinary semi-local stretch 𝑠 = √ 𝜌+/𝜇+. (b)

Figure 2: Wall-layer length scales and finite-amplitude density correction. (a) Wall-normal range: ℓ/(𝜅𝑌+) gives the near-wall damping of the elliptic length scale and 𝑊 (𝑌+) gives the outer weighting of the density term. The shaded band marks the buffer-layer range and the vertical marker indicates 𝑌+ 𝑜 = 70, where 𝑊 = 1/2. (b) Density-correction function,

Table 1: DNS flow conditions used to set constants and assess the elliptic semi-local transformation. The cases progress from cooled attached boundary layers to independent high-speed heat-transfer data, station-resolved fields, mixed thermal-wall channels and a non-equilibrium shock interaction. Reynolds and Mach numbers follow the notation of the source DNS studies.

Figure 3: Transformation sequence for the Zhang-Duan-Choudhari smooth high-speed ZPG flat plates (Zhang et al. 2018). (a) Mean velocity profiles in ordinary wall units (𝑦+, 𝑢+). (b) The same profiles after Trettel-Larsson and GFM transformations, plotted as transformed velocity 𝑈+ versus transformed coordinate 𝑌+. (c) Profiles after the present coordinate and velocity transformation, plotted as (𝑌+,𝑈+). Colours in panels (a) and (c) identify Mach number and wall thermal condition; colours in panel (b) identify the transformation. Dashed and dotted black curves denote the viscous law and the classical logarithmic law.

Figure 4: Transformed-coordinate field on the Mach 2.28 ZPG boundary-layer mean field from the Volpiani-Bernardini-Larsson data (Volpiani et al. 2018, 2023) before station profiles are selected, plotted against streamwise station 𝑥/𝛿𝑖 and wall-normal distance 𝑦+. (a) Mean density field 𝜌+. (b) Elliptic coordinate correction 𝜙. (c) Transformed coordinate 𝑌+ with selected coordinate contours. (d) Transformed streamwise velocity 𝑈+. The lower boundary is the wall; the same wall condition, wall-distance normal and outer-boundary flux conditions define the transformed coordinate and velocity from which station profiles are read.

Figure 5: Primary boundary-layer method comparison. Columns show Trettel-Larsson, GFM and the present transformation. (a-c) Attached high-speed ZPG flat-plate profiles from Zhang et al. (2018). (d-f) Station-resolved Mach 2.28 ZPG boundary-layer profiles from the Volpiani-Bernardini-Larsson data (Volpiani et al. 2018, 2023). Profiles are plotted as transformed velocity versus transformed coordinate. Colours identify the Mach number and wall thermal condition of the Zhang-Duan-Choudhari cases in the upper row and the station groups of the Mach 2.28 cases in the lower row, as labelled in the panels. Dashed and dotted black curves denote the viscous law and the classical logarithmic law.

Figure 6: Cogo high-speed ZPG boundary-layer comparison (Cogo et al. 2023a,b). Panels separate (a) 𝑀∞ = 2-4 and (b) 𝑀∞ ≃ 5.86-6 Cogo cases. Each panel compares Trettel-Larsson (green dashed), GFM (blue solid) and the present transformation (orange solid). Profiles are plotted as transformed velocity versus transformed coordinate. Dashed and dotted black curves denote the viscous law and the classical logarithmic law.

Figure 7: Coordinate correction through the cooled-wall shock/boundary-layer interaction of Hirai-Kawai. (a) Mean density normalised by the wall value, 𝜌/𝜌𝑤 , over (𝑥 − 𝑥𝑠ℎ)/𝐿𝑖𝑛𝑡 ≃ −3.5 to 4.3; the shaded interval marks the interaction and early recovery region, and the dash-dotted line marks shock impingement. (b) Near-wall coordinate correction 𝜙 over the same streamwise range, with clipped colour limits. Triangular markers in (a,b) denote the station profiles shown in (c,d). (c) Mean velocity profiles in ordinary wall units (𝑦+, 𝑢+). (d) Transformed profiles (𝑌+,𝑈+). The colour of each profile is tied to its station marker and to the colour bar in (a,b), moving from upstream influence through the interaction and early recovery region in increasing (𝑥 − 𝑥𝑠ℎ)/𝐿𝑖𝑛𝑡 .

Figure 8: Mixed thermal-wall channel formulation with density measured from the centreline. (a,b) Transformed velocity profiles on the isothermal and adiabatic sides, respectively, compared using Trettel-Larsson, GFM and the present transformation; panel (b) uses the same method colours and line styles as panel (a). In panel (b), the relevant reference is the viscous relation 𝑈+ = 𝑌+, because the adiabatic-side comparison concerns near-wall and buffer-layer displacement rather than logarithmic-layer collapse. (c) Density change relative to the centreline, 𝑆𝜌 = log(𝜌/𝜌𝑐), for the two sides. (d) Coordinate stretching 𝑠𝑌 = 𝑠 exp 𝜙 from the present elliptic coordinate equation. Profiles use transformed coordinates on each channel half.
